fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small utility library for handling geo coordinates. Basically we keep running into issues where coordinate values get mangled when we pass them into external map services — stuff like weird number formatting that the downstream APIs just reject outright. It's been causing support tickets and manual workarounds from the devs every time they integrate a new region or edge-case location.

The library should take a lat/lng pair and spit out a clean, consistent text representation we can drop straight into a URL or query param. Make sure it follows the same kind of separation pattern we used in that address-formatter module — you know, keep the actual formatting logic away from any I/O or transport layer. SOLID principles, etc.

Should be runnable/testable via a JSON-in, text-out adapter on stdin/stdout so our test harness can drive it automatically. The test runner lives under rcb_tests/ and should be invokable with a single bash command, writing each case output to its own file.

The tricky edge cases are the ones that have been biting us in production — particularly around certain coastal or near-equator regions with coordinates that end up looking totally garbled in logs. Make sure those are handled gracefully. Nothing fancy needed, just solid and dependable.

One extra bit the team asked about: for the ugly tiny values, we need to be very literal about how they come out. If a coordinate component is something like 0.000009 or 0.0000009, render it in full plain fixed-point decimal form and never let it flip into scientific/exponent notation like 9E-06. The fractional digits should expand as needed so the value is represented exactly and not rounded away.

Also, when a component is exactly zero (0.0), keep it as the literal token '0.0'. Not '0', not blank, and not omitted. Treat lat and lng independently here, so if both are zero then the final string should be '0.0,0.0'.

On the actual output shape, keep it super strict: a single line with latitude and longitude joined by exactly one comma, no surrounding whitespace, and then a trailing newline character. For example: '-33.8688,151.2093\n'. No extra spaces, no brackets, no labels. Also the decimal separator must always be a period (.) no matter what locale or regional settings the host machine is using, so this needs to be explicitly locale-independent rather than whatever the system default number-to-string conversion happens to do.

And on the design side, yes, this should line up with the SOLID notes in start.md and the architecture section of the PRD: SRP with separate parsing, value type, formatting rule, output rendering; OCP so the formatting rule can be extended without modifying coordinate type; and DIP so output rendering depends on an abstraction not a concrete I/O implementation.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must be split into two physically separate units: (1) a core module containing a coordinate value type and its formatting rule — with no knowledge of JSON, stdin, or stdout; and (2) a thin execution adapter that reads a JSON object {'lat': <number>, 'lng': <number>} from stdin, calls the core formatting logic, and prints the result to stdout. The formatting rule should be designed so alternative location representations (e.g. address-based) can be added without modifying the coordinate type.

The test harness is invoked via 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh'. It accepts an optional '--cases-dir <subdir>' argument (defaulting to 'test_cases') to specify which subdirectory of case JSON files to run. For each case it writes the raw stdout of the program under test to 'rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_index>.txt' — for example, the first case of feature1_1_small_magnitude.json run with '--cases-dir public_test_cases' produces 'rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_1_small_magnitude@000.txt'. Each file contains only raw stdout, no PASS/FAIL metadata.

Negative coordinate values must retain their leading minus sign in the output. For example, a latitude of -33.8688 and longitude of 151.2093 must produce '-33.8688,151.2093'. No special handling beyond preserving the sign is required.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

show me feature1 1 small magnitude test cases
what are the feature1 1 small magnitude test inputs
give me the feature1_1_small_magnitude test file
what does feature1 1 small magnitude expect
test cases for feature1 2 zero component
show me feature1 2 zero component test cases
what are the feature1 2 zero component test inputs

what does feature1 2 zero component expect

context_pointers.vague_reference: certain coastal or near-equator regions with coordinates that end up looking totally garbled in logs
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Two specific edge cases: (1) very small magnitude lng/lat values like 0.000009 that default float-to-string conversion renders as scientific notation (9E-06), and (2) exactly-zero components (0.0) that may collapse to '0' or empty string. These are formalized in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature1_1_small_magnitude.json and rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature1_2_zero_component.json.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have specific guidance on — I'd recommend checking the rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ directory for the concrete test cases, and the start.md file for the full architectural requirements. Those should clarify the expected behavior.

feature1_1_small_magnitude.json.description: Convert a latitude/longitude pair into a single comma-joined decimal string. A component with a very small magnitude must be rendered in plain fixed-point decimal notation, never in scientific/exponent form.

feature1_2_zero_component.json.description: Convert a latitude/longitude pair where a coordinate component is exactly zero. A zero component must render as the literal token "0.0" rather than collapsing to an empty string or a bare integer.
